include: Published libxml2 CVEs whose primary issue is not memory corruption

Issues whose root cause can survive a memory-safe Rust rewrite, especially entity-expansion policy bugs, XXE exposure, uncontrolled recursion, algorithmic denial of service, decompression-resource bugs, and parser/XPath/schema logic or invariant failures
exclude: C-only memory-corruption issues whose security impact depends on raw pointer misuse, manual buffer sizing, or use-after-free
Wrapper-specific or tool-only issues that do not reflect the core libxml2 library semantics being ported
Disputed or too-vague records where the public description does not establish a concrete porting-relevant bug class

notes: The selected set is intentionally narrower than all non-memory bugs in the corpus.

The dominant Rust-port risks are unsafe XML feature defaults, missing expansion and recursion limits, algorithmic denial of service, decompression loop/resource handling, and logic bugs that would become panic-or-error-propagation failures in Rust.

porting_themes.theme: Entity Expansion And External Entity Policy
guidance: Make external entity, DTD, network, file, and catalog resolution explicit opt-in behavior.
Apply depth, breadth, and total-expanded-byte budgets across all entity-processing paths, including attribute values, reader mode, SAX mode, and recovery mode.
Add regression tests for billion-laughs, parameter-entity recursion, reader/XEE constant-memory regressions, and XXE bypass variants.
Centralize recursion-depth accounting for parser, XPath, schema, RelaxNG, and catalog evaluation code.
Do not reset depth counters during recursive re-entry; treat over-limit conditions as normal parse errors.
Prefer iterative traversal where practical, and keep the same limits enabled in recovery and compatibility modes.
Bound decompressor memory, total output size, and loop iterations when handling compressed input.
Treat decompressor memlimit and format errors as terminal failures, not retryable states.
Test malformed and truncated XZ/LZMA inputs for hang-free and panic-free behavior.
Treat attacker-controlled invalid states in XPath, schema, and parser internals as recoverable `Result` errors.
Avoid `panic!`, `unwrap()`, `expect()`, and `unreachable!` on parser states that malformed input can influence.
Fuzz invalid XPath, XSD, mixed-content, and entity-decoding cases specifically for crash-free error handling.
Use randomized or collision-resistant hashing for attacker-controlled names and dictionary keys.
Cap symbol-table growth and worst-case lookup behavior per document or parsing context.

relevant_cves.primary_impact: Denial of service via CPU and memory exhaustion from nested entity expansion
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Missing recursion and expansion limits for entity substitution
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Rust does not change XML entity-expansion semantics; a Rust port still needs explicit recursion and total-expansion budgets.

relevant_cves.primary_impact: Denial of service via infinite loop on invalid UTF-8 input
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Parser progress bug on malformed multibyte sequences
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Memory safety does not prevent non-terminating decode loops; the Rust port must guarantee forward progress or fail fast on malformed encodings.
relevant_cves.local_repo_evidence: original/debian/changelog:1272 notes the xmlCurrentChar infinite-loop fix

relevant_cves.primary_impact: Denial of service via CPU and memory exhaustion from recursive entity expansion in attributes
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Missing recursion and expansion limits in a secondary entity-processing path
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: A Rust rewrite can still miss expansion checks in less common parser paths such as attribute decoding.
relevant_cves.local_repo_evidence: original/debian/changelog:1213 records the upstream fix for this attribute-expansion case

relevant_cves.primary_impact: Denial of service via crafted predefined-entity definitions causing unbounded memory use
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Entity-processing logic bug distinct from the classic billion-laughs cases
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: A memory-safe implementation can still preserve incorrect entity semantics and allow unbounded expansion or allocation.

relevant_cves.primary_impact: Denial of service via stack exhaustion from deeply nested element declarations
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Unbounded recursion on attacker-controlled DTD structure
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Safe Rust still allows attacker-driven recursion and stack overflow unless the port tracks and limits nesting depth.
relevant_cves.local_repo_evidence: original/debian/changelog:1162 mentions the deep DTD declaration fix

relevant_cves.primary_impact: Denial of service via predictable hash-collision CPU exhaustion
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Deterministic hashing for attacker-controlled keys
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: A Rust port remains exposed if it keeps libxml2-style deterministic hashing instead of randomized or collision-resistant hashing for untrusted names.
relevant_cves.local_repo_evidence: original/debian/changelog:956 explicitly says dictionaries were changed to mitigate hash DoS

relevant_cves.primary_impact: Denial of service via linear-complexity internal entity expansion
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Entity replacement cost was attacker-amplifiable even without classic exponential recursion
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Entity expansion remains a semantic problem in any implementation language, so the Rust port needs explicit work and size budgets.

relevant_cves.primary_impact: XXE leading to file disclosure, SSRF-style outbound requests, and resource exhaustion
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Insecure default behavior and API ergonomics around external entities
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: A Rust port can reproduce the same insecure defaults unless external entity loading is disabled by default and dangerous APIs are hard to use accidentally.

relevant_cves.primary_impact: Unwanted external fetches and resource consumption despite parser flags
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Flag interaction bug that fetched external parameter entities when the caller expected them disabled
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Safe Rust does not help if parser configuration is interpreted incorrectly; the port needs a single authoritative policy gate for all external-resource loads.
relevant_cves.local_repo_evidence: original/doc/news.html:961 says the fix was to stop fetching external parameter entities

relevant_cves.primary_impact: Billion-laughs-style denial of service even when entity substitution was disabled
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Protection bypass in entity-expansion gating
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: A Rust port can still get the enable/disable semantics wrong and silently re-open expansion paths the caller believes are blocked.
relevant_cves.local_repo_evidence: original/doc/news.html:960 describes this as a billion-laughs variant; original/doc/news.html:939 notes a follow-up regression fix

relevant_cves.primary_impact: Denial of service via entity expansion breaking constant-memory behavior
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Streaming API failed to preserve its resource-usage guarantees under crafted input
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: A Rust streaming parser can still lose its intended memory bounds if entity expansion and buffering rules are not enforced consistently.
relevant_cves.local_repo_evidence: original/doc/news.html:899 says the fix was to enforce constant-memory behavior in the reader

relevant_cves.primary_impact: Denial of service via another entity-expansion bypass
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Expansion limits were incomplete in xmlStringLenDecodeEntities
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Entity safety checks must be centralized; otherwise a Rust port can fix one expansion path and leave another equivalent helper function exposed.
relevant_cves.local_repo_evidence: original/doc/news.html:893 labels this as another entity-expansion issue

relevant_cves.primary_impact: Denial of service via process hang on crafted compressed input
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Decompression error handling loop failed to terminate
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Rust memory safety does not prevent decompressor state machines from spinning forever when error conditions are mishandled.
relevant_cves.local_repo_evidence: original/doc/news.html:896 says the XZ compression support loop was fixed

relevant_cves.primary_impact: Denial of service via infinite recursion and stack exhaustion
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Recursive entity construction remained possible in recovery mode
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Compatibility and recovery modes need the same recursion limits as strict parsing; otherwise a Rust port can reintroduce stack-exhaustion bugs on its error-tolerant paths.
relevant_cves.local_repo_evidence: original/debian/changelog:722 says the fix was to avoid building recursive entities

relevant_cves.primary_impact: Denial of service via stack exhaustion from nested entity references
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Recursion-depth accounting bug in entity-related parser functions
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: A Rust port still needs correct shared depth accounting across mutually recursive parsing helpers.
relevant_cves.local_repo_evidence: original/debian/changelog:707 records the upstream security fix for this recursion-depth issue

relevant_cves.primary_impact: XXE-based file disclosure and resource exhaustion
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Entity content was fetched even when the parsing mode should have avoided external data access
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: A Rust port can repeat the same policy bypass if entity decoding helpers are allowed to resolve external content without a central permission check.
relevant_cves.local_repo_evidence: original/debian/changelog:716 calls this an inappropriate fetch of entity content

relevant_cves.primary_impact: Makes XXE prevention harder by lacking a same-document-only policy control
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Insufficient security-relevant parser option surface
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: This is directly a Rust API-design concern: the port should expose safe-by-default resolution policies instead of forcing callers to choose between too-broad options.
relevant_cves.local_repo_evidence: original/debian/changelog:459 describes this as an XXE fix

relevant_cves.primary_impact: XML entity inclusion with local-file and network exposure under default-like settings
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Insecure default or unexpectedly reachable external-entity behavior
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: A memory-safe port can still ship the wrong defaults; external resolution should be opt-in and auditable.
relevant_cves.local_repo_evidence: original/debian/changelog:589 says the fix prevents unwanted external entity references

relevant_cves.primary_impact: Denial of service via infinite recursion
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Missing recursion limit for parameter-entity expansion
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Parameter entities are a separate expansion surface that needs the same recursion accounting as general entities in a Rust port.
relevant_cves.local_repo_evidence: original/debian/changelog:478 records the parameter-entity recursion fix

relevant_cves.primary_impact: Denial of service via excessive memory allocation
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: No decompressor memory limit matched to legitimate input needs
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Rust does not automatically bound attacker-driven allocation in decompression code; the port still needs explicit memory budgets.
relevant_cves.local_repo_evidence: original/debian/changelog:480 says a memory limit was added for LZMA decompression

relevant_cves.primary_impact: Denial of service via infinite loop after LZMA memlimit errors
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Compressed-input error state did not terminate parsing
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: The Rust port needs explicit error-state transitions for compressed streams; safe code can still loop forever on malformed data.
relevant_cves.local_repo_evidence: original/doc/news.html:504 groups this with a fix for infinite loops in LZMA decompression

relevant_cves.primary_impact: Denial of service via a second infinite-loop variant after LZMA memlimit errors
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Another compressed-input control-flow bug in the same feature area
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: This shows the need for systematic decompressor-state testing, not just one targeted fix, in a Rust port.
relevant_cves.local_repo_evidence: original/doc/news.html:504 groups this with CVE-2018-9251 under infinite-loop fixes

relevant_cves.primary_impact: Denial of service via crash on invalid XPath logical expressions
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Logic and invariant bug in XPath operand handling
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: In Rust this bug class would likely become a panic or aborted evaluation if invalid intermediate states are treated as impossible rather than as parse errors.
relevant_cves.local_repo_evidence: original/doc/news.html:505 says the fix addressed a nullptr deref in XPath logic ops

relevant_cves.primary_impact: Denial of service via infinite loop on crafted end-of-file conditions
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Parser progress bug in xmlStringLenDecodeEntities
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Forward-progress bugs are language-agnostic; the Rust port must ensure every malformed-input path either consumes input or terminates with an error.
relevant_cves.local_repo_evidence: original/debian/changelog:399 records the infinite-loop fix in xmlStringLenDecodeEntities

relevant_cves.primary_impact: Denial of service via crash after parser errors were not propagated
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Error-propagation bug leading to invalid internal state
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: A Rust port can reproduce this as `unwrap()` or `expect()` failures if it does not carry parser errors through later validation stages.
relevant_cves.local_repo_evidence: original/debian/changelog:298 says the fix was to propagate the parser error

relevant_cves.primary_impact: Denial of service via bypass of existing exponential-entity-expansion protections
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Incomplete protection logic for hostile entity graphs
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: It is not enough to port one anti-expansion check; the Rust implementation needs defense-in-depth against variants that bypass the original guard rails.
relevant_cves.local_repo_evidence: original/NEWS and original/doc/news.html both record a dedicated patch for this security issue

relevant_cves.primary_impact: Denial of service via crash on invalid schema fixup
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Nullability and invariant bug in schema post-processing
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: A Rust port can still panic on invalid schema states if schema fixup code assumes earlier passes established stronger invariants than they actually did.
relevant_cves.local_repo_evidence: original/debian/changelog:166 and the Debian patch both describe a null-deref fix in xmlSchemaFixupComplexType

relevant_cves.primary_impact: Classic XXE despite custom SAX handlers trying to suppress external entity content
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Callback contract and entity-resolution semantics were inconsistent
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: A Rust SAX API can repeat this if callback-side suppression is advisory instead of enforced by the parser's actual entity-resolution path.

relevant_cves.primary_impact: Denial of service via stack exhaustion from uncontrolled recursion
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Recursion-depth counter was reset during recursive XPath calls
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Recursive evaluators in Rust need stable shared depth accounting across re-entry; otherwise safe code still overflows the stack.
relevant_cves.local_repo_evidence: original/debian/changelog:42 says the patch makes the XPath depth check work with recursive calls

relevant_cves.primary_impact: Denial of service via null-dereference crash in xmlPatMatch
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Input-triggered invalid-state handling bug in pattern evaluation
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: The public description is sparse, but this still looks like a core parser-logic nullability bug that could become a Rust panic if invalid states are not modeled explicitly.

relevant_cves.primary_impact: Denial of service via null-dereference crash on crafted XPath-bearing input
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Input-triggered logic bug in XPath evaluation
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Even without memory-unsafety, XPath evaluators can still panic or abort on malformed expressions if absence and error states are not propagated carefully.

relevant_cves.primary_impact: Denial of service via excessive recursion and stack exhaustion
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: No inclusion-depth limit for nested RelaxNG `<include>` chains
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Schema inclusion depth is a semantic budget the Rust port must enforce explicitly; safe stack frames still exhaust.
relevant_cves.local_repo_evidence: original/debian/changelog:10 says the patch added an inclusion limit helper in RelaxNG parsing

relevant_cves.primary_impact: Denial of service via self-referential catalog delegate recursion
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Catalog traversal lacked depth checks and cycle resistance
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Rust code still needs explicit cycle and depth detection when walking attacker-controlled catalog graphs.
relevant_cves.local_repo_evidence: original/debian/changelog:20 says the patch added MAX_CATAL_DEPTH and related checks

relevant_cves.primary_impact: Denial of service via excessive CPU consumption from repeated `nextCatalog` traversal
relevant_cves.root_cause_class: Resource-consumption bug caused by duplicate or cyclical catalog traversal
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Catalog processing remains vulnerable to algorithmic blowups unless the Rust port deduplicates and budgets downstream traversals.
relevant_cves.local_repo_evidence: original/debian/changelog:24 says the patch added catalog duplication checks

notable_excluded_non_memory_cves.reason: These are PHP `xsl_ext_function_php` wrapper bugs, not core libxml2 parser or library-semantics bugs.
These are downstream regression or packaging issues around previously known recursion bugs rather than distinct upstream bug classes to carry into the Rust port.
The public record says parameter-entity references were mishandled but does not describe a concrete impact or bug class precisely enough to map into Rust porting guidance.
Recover-mode-only null dereference in a manual-recovery path; included in the corpus but lower-priority and partly disputed by the maintainer.
These are memory leaks from missed release paths. They matter operationally but are more tied to C lifetime management than to a likely semantic bug class in a safe Rust rewrite.
This record applies to `lxml` code paths that combine libxml2 with wrapper logic, not to libxml2 alone.
Disputed, local-only SGML catalog recursion in `xmlcatalog`; low-confidence and low-relevance for the planned core library port.
Whitespace-triggered memory leak in the interactive `xmllint` shell; tool-only and not representative of core library semantics.
